questions.title: Heart Failure Assessment
questions.text: A nurse is assessing a client with heart failure. Which assessment finding would indicate right-sided heart failure?
questions.rationale: Neck vein distention (jugular venous distention) is a classic sign of right-sided heart failure. When the right ventricle fails to pump effectively, blood backs up into the right atrium and the venous system, causing distention of the jugular veins. Pulmonary crackles (rales) occur in left-sided heart failure due to fluid backup into the pulmonary system. Paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea (waking up at night with shortness of breath) is a symptom of left-sided heart failure caused by redistribution of fluid when lying flat. An S3 heart sound (ventricular gallop) is often heard in left-sided heart failure and indicates volume overload of the ventricle.

questions.title: Cardiac Medication Effects
questions.text: A client is prescribed lisinopril for hypertension. Which laboratory value should the nurse monitor closely during treatment?
questions.rationale: Serum potassium levels should be monitored closely in clients taking lisinopril, an angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitor. ACE inhibitors can increase potassium levels by decreasing aldosterone production, which can lead to hyperkalemia, especially in clients with renal impairment or those taking potassium supplements or potassium-sparing diuretics. Hemoglobin levels are not specifically affected by ACE inhibitors. Platelet count is not typically affected by ACE inhibitors. Serum calcium levels are not significantly affected by ACE inhibitors.

questions.title: Coronary Artery Disease
questions.text: A client with stable angina is preparing for discharge. Which instruction should the nurse include in the teaching plan?

choices.text: "Take nitroglycerin after chest pain has been present for 15 minutes."
"Avoid all physical activity to prevent angina episodes."
"Take nitroglycerin before activities that may cause angina."
"Call emergency services if angina pain is relieved by nitroglycerin."

questions.rationale: The nurse should instruct the client to take nitroglycerin prophylactically before engaging in activities known to trigger angina attacks. This preventive use can help avoid angina episodes. Waiting 15 minutes after chest pain begins before taking nitroglycerin is too long and could lead to myocardial damage; nitroglycerin should be taken as soon as angina pain begins. Avoiding all physical activity is not recommended for clients with stable angina; instead, a regular, moderate exercise program under medical supervision is usually beneficial. The client should call emergency services if angina is NOT relieved by nitroglycerin (after taking up to 3 doses, 5 minutes apart), not if it is relieved, which would be the expected therapeutic response.

questions.title: Valvular Heart Disease
questions.text: A nurse is caring for a client with mitral stenosis. Which assessment finding would the nurse expect to observe?
questions.rationale: A diastolic murmur at the apex (mitral area) is characteristic of mitral stenosis. In mitral stenosis, the narrowed mitral valve impedes blood flow from the left atrium to the left ventricle during diastole, creating a rumbling, low-pitched diastolic murmur best heard at the apex. Bounding peripheral pulses are associated with aortic regurgitation or high-output states, not mitral stenosis, which typically presents with weak, thready pulses due to decreased cardiac output. Wide pulse pressure (the difference between systolic and diastolic blood pressure) is associated with aortic regurgitation, not mitral stenosis. Barrel chest is a physical finding associated with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), not primarily with mitral stenosis, although mitral stenosis can lead to pulmonary hypertension and right heart failure over time.

questions.title: Hypertension Management
questions.text: A nurse is teaching a client newly diagnosed with hypertension about lifestyle modifications. Which statement by the client indicates understanding of the teaching?

choices.text: "I should limit my sodium intake to less than 2,300 mg per day."
"I need to eliminate all fats from my diet."
"I should avoid all physical activity to prevent blood pressure elevation."
"I can continue smoking but should limit it to less than 5 cigarettes per day."

questions.rationale: Limiting sodium intake to less than 2,300 mg per day (and ideally less than 1,500 mg per day for many adults) is a recommended lifestyle modification for hypertension management. Sodium restriction helps reduce blood volume and thus blood pressure. The client should not eliminate all fats from the diet, but rather focus on reducing saturated and trans fats while including healthy fats like those found in olive oil, avocados, and fatty fish. Regular physical activity is recommended for hypertension management; moderate-intensity aerobic activity for at least 150 minutes per week can help lower blood pressure. Complete smoking cessation, not reduction, is recommended for hypertension management and overall cardiovascular health.

questions.title: Cardiac Dysrhythmias
questions.text: A nurse is caring for a client experiencing new-onset atrial fibrillation. Which immediate intervention should the nurse anticipate?
questions.rationale: Administration of a beta-blocker is an appropriate immediate intervention for new-onset atrial fibrillation to control ventricular rate. Beta-blockers slow atrioventricular (AV) node conduction, reducing the rapid ventricular response often seen in atrial fibrillation. Cardioversion (electrical or pharmacological) may be considered for rhythm control but is not typically the first-line intervention for stable patients with new-onset atrial fibrillation. A permanent pacemaker is not typically indicated for atrial fibrillation; it is used for bradyarrhythmias like complete heart block or sick sinus syndrome. Atropine would be contraindicated in atrial fibrillation as it increases heart rate by blocking parasympathetic influence on the heart; it is used for symptomatic bradycardia.

questions.title: Peripheral Vascular Disease
questions.text: A nurse is assessing a client with peripheral arterial disease. Which finding would be most consistent with this diagnosis?
questions.rationale: Intermittent claudication, characterized by muscle pain or cramping in the legs triggered by activity and relieved by rest, is the hallmark symptom of peripheral arterial disease (PAD). It results from inadequate arterial blood flow to the muscles during activity. Warm, erythematous extremities would suggest inflammation or infection, not PAD, which typically presents with cool, pale extremities due to decreased arterial perfusion. Edema of the lower extremities is more consistent with venous insufficiency or heart failure than with PAD, which is an arterial problem. Visible varicosities (varicose veins) are associated with venous insufficiency, not PAD.

questions.title: Myocardial Infarction Complications
questions.text: A client is in the acute phase of a myocardial infarction. Which complication should the nurse monitor for in the first 24 hours?
questions.rationale: Dysrhythmias are the most common complication in the first 24 hours following a myocardial infarction (MI). They result from electrical disturbances caused by ischemia, necrosis, reperfusion injury, autonomic imbalance, or electrolyte abnormalities. Dressler's syndrome (post-MI syndrome) is a late complication that typically develops 1-6 weeks after an MI and involves pericardial inflammation. Left ventricular aneurysm is a late complication that occurs weeks to months after an MI when the necrotic tissue is replaced by scar tissue that bulges during systole. While papillary muscle rupture can occur within the first few days after an MI (particularly 2-7 days), dysrhythmias are more common within the first 24 hours.

questions.title: Cardiac Rehabilitation
questions.text: A nurse is planning discharge for a client who had a myocardial infarction. Which component of cardiac rehabilitation should the nurse emphasize?

choices.text: Bed rest for 4-6 weeks to allow the heart to heal
Gradual increase in physical activity based on tolerance
Avoidance of sexual activity for at least 6 months
Immediate return to pre-infarction level of activity

questions.rationale: Gradual increase in physical activity based on tolerance is the appropriate approach for cardiac rehabilitation after a myocardial infarction. This progressive approach helps improve cardiovascular fitness while minimizing risk. Prolonged bed rest (4-6 weeks) is not recommended and can lead to deconditioning, increased risk of thromboembolism, and other complications; current guidelines recommend early mobilization. Complete avoidance of sexual activity for 6 months is unnecessarily restrictive; most clients can resume sexual activity within a few weeks when they can perform moderate physical activity without symptoms. Immediate return to pre-infarction level of activity could put excessive strain on the healing heart and is not recommended; instead, a gradual, supervised increase in activity is the standard approach.

questions.title: Anticoagulant Therapy
questions.text: A client is prescribed warfarin (Coumadin) for atrial fibrillation. Which statement by the client indicates a need for further teaching?

choices.text: "I should take my medication at the same time each day."
"I will notify all healthcare providers about my warfarin therapy."
"I can take ibuprofen for occasional pain relief."
"I should report unusual bleeding or bruising to my healthcare provider."

questions.rationale: The statement "I can take ibuprofen for occasional pain relief" indicates a need for further teaching. Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) like ibuprofen can increase the risk of bleeding when taken with warfarin by affecting platelet function and potentially causing gastric irritation or ulceration. Acetaminophen is generally the preferred analgesic for clients on warfarin. Taking warfarin at the same time each day helps maintain consistent blood levels. Notifying all healthcare providers about warfarin therapy is important to prevent potential drug interactions and manage anticoagulation during procedures. Reporting unusual bleeding or bruising is crucial for early detection of excessive anticoagulation.
